message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Latency/responsiveness is a decisive factor: screen reader users rapidly skip and interrupt speech, so the engine must begin producing audio immediately and restart instantly, whereas neural engines like Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot start until a full text chunk is available."

**Source:** https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Quote any text about screen reader users skipping/interrupting speech and engines needing to start producing audio immediately.

content.content:
I cannot provide the information you're requesting. The webpage content provided only shows a GitHub discussion index page with a link to an article titled "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users," but the actual article content is not included in the material you've shared.

To answer your questions about latency, responsiveness, streaming capabilities, and specific details regarding Supertonic and Kitten TTS engines, I would need access to the full text of the article at `stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html`, which is not present in the webpage excerpt you provided.

Web search results for query: "Supertonic Kitten TTS streaming latency screen reader begin generating speech chunk"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - supertone-inc/supertonic: Lightning-Fast, On-Device, Multilingual TTS — running natively via ONNX.","url":"https://github.com/supertone-inc/supertonic"},{"title":"Supertonic 3 TTS: Lightning Fast On-Device Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://supertonictts.com/"},{"title":"Supertonic TTS: Lightning Fast On-Device Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://supertonic-tts.com/"},{"title":"supertonic tts turkish language and screen readers · Issue #144 · supertone-inc/supertonic","url":"https://github.com/supertone-inc/supertonic/issues/144"},{"title":"GitHub - devnen/Kitten-TTS-Server: Self-host the ultra-lightweight Kitten TTS model with this enhanced API server with an intuitive Web UI, large text processing for audiobooks, and GPU acceleration. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/devnen/Kitten-TTS-Server"},{"title":"Supertone/supertonic · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/Supertone/supertonic"},{"title":"supertonic · GitHub Topics · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/topics/supertonic"},{"title":"Kitten TTS — Lightweight Browser Text to Speech | OfflineTTS","url":"https://offlinetts.com/app/kitten/"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about both Supertonic TTS and Kitten TTS in relation to screen readers and streaming latency:

## Supertonic TTS and Screen Reader Integration

A visually impaired user developed a Supertonic TTS driver for the NVDA screen reader, but reported that when using it with screen readers, there are significant delays and stutters, with the screen reader's voice responses being really slow and text synthesized much too late, making Supertonic extremely slow when used with screen readers.

## Streaming and Latency Characteristics

Since all processing happens on-device, there is no network latency, and speech generation begins immediately when you provide text input, making it ideal for interactive applications.

## Kitten TTS

The KittenTTS model by KittenML provides a foundation for generating high-quality speech from models smaller than 80MB, and can be elevated into a production-ready service by providing a robust FastAPI server that makes KittenTTS significantly easier to use, more powerful, and drastically faster.

Kitten TTS intelligently splits long texts into manageable chunks, processes them individually and seamlessly concatenates the resulting audio.

The search results show that while Supertonic TTS offers low latency for on-device processing, users have reported latency challenges when integrating it with screen readers specifically, though chunked streaming approaches like those in Kitten TTS servers can help with processing longer texts.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The primary source's actual content is in the linked article. Let me fetch that article directly and also look at the Supertonic issue about screen readers.

input.prompt: What does this article say about latency, responsiveness, streaming, and the ability of TTS engines (especially Supertonic and Kitten TTS) to begin generating speech before a full text chunk is available? Quote any text about screen reader users skipping/interrupting speech, restarting instantly, and engines needing to start producing audio immediately versus needing an entire chunk of text first.
What does this issue say about Supertonic TTS latency, responsiveness, and delays when used with screen readers? Does it say the engine cannot start until a full chunk of text is available?

content.content:
# TTS Latency and Streaming in Screen Reader Context

## Key Findings on Responsiveness

The article emphasizes that modern AI TTS systems fail to meet screen reader users' need for immediate speech generation. Regarding streaming capabilities:

**Supertonic's Advantage:** The system "can stream result audio as it becomes available," providing faster initial response compared to Kitten TTS, which "cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated."

## Chunk Processing Limitation

Both models share a critical flaw: "they cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text." This contrasts sharply with screen reader requirements.

## User Interruption Needs

The author explains why this matters for blind users:

> "Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech."

## The Core Problem

Screen reader users need immediate audio output and rapid restart capability, but modern AI TTS systems introduce latency by requiring full text chunks before beginning synthesis—a fundamental incompatibility with actual usage patterns in accessibility contexts.

# Supertonic TTS Latency Issues with Screen Readers

Based on the issue, the user reports significant performance problems when using Supertonic with screen readers. They state: "When using it with screen readers, there are significant delays and stutters."

The user further describes the problem as: "The screen reader's voice responses are really slow; the text I type or try to have read aloud is synthesized much too late."

However, **the issue does not mention anything about the engine requiring a full chunk of text before starting synthesis**. The complaint focuses on overall slowness and delayed responses during screen reader usage, but doesn't specify the technical cause—such as chunking behavior or buffering requirements.

content.text:
The primary source directly supports the claim. Let me verify the nuance: the claim says "neural engines like Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot start until a full text chunk is available" — but the article notes Supertonic CAN stream audio as it becomes available (within a chunk), while Kitten cannot. Both still need a full chunk of text before starting. Let me confirm this distinction is correctly captured and check whether the claim overreaches on the streaming point.

The key quotes from the primary source:
1. "they cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text" — directly supports the claim about both engines needing a full text chunk.
2. "Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech" — supports the skip/interrupt/restart point.
3. Supertonic "can stream result audio as it becomes available" but still needs the full chunk of text first.

The claim is accurately supported by the quote and the surrounding article context. The one subtlety — that Supertonic streams output audio while Kitten doesn't — concerns output streaming, but both still require a full input text chunk to begin, which is exactly what the claim states. Let me do a quick sanity check on the dates and whether this is a credible primary source for the accessibility/screen-reader community perspective.

Based on the web search results, I found the article you were looking for along with related information about modern AI text-to-speech systems for screen reader users:

## Main Article Found

The article "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users" is hosted on interfree.ca, which appears to be the primary source matching your search query.

## Key Findings from the Article

The article discusses several important limitations of modern AI-based TTS systems when used for screen readers:

**Accuracy Issues:** Both models tested had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints from text punctuation. When it comes to use in a screen reader, skipping words, or reading numbers incorrectly, is unacceptable.

**Speed and Latency Challenges:** Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text. Supertonic is slightly faster, as it can stream result audio as it becomes available, whereas Kitten TTS cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated.

The article examines how modern neural network-based TTS systems compare to traditional approaches in meeting the practical needs of screen reader users, particularly regarding the critical factors of accuracy, speed, and latency.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have full confirmation. The search result independently corroborates the exact quote from the primary source: "Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text. Supertonic is slightly faster, as it can stream result audio as it becomes available, whereas Kitten TTS cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated."

Let me assess the claim against the checklist:

1. **Supported by quote?** Yes. The article explicitly states both engines "cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text" and that screen reader users "quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech." Both parts of the claim are directly and accurately supported.

2. **Contradicting evidence?** No credible source contradicts the core point. There is one minor nuance: Supertonic CAN stream output audio as it's generated (so it has lower latency to first audio than Kitten). The claim lumps them together as "cannot start until a full text chunk is available" — but that is about INPUT text chunking, which is true for both. The claim is accurate on the input-chunk point. It does not falsely claim Supertonic can't stream output. So no overreach.

3. **Source quality?** This is a firsthand, technically-detailed account from a screen-reader power user (the exact community the research question wants), discussed on the official espeak-ng GitHub org and surfaced on Hacker News. Appropriate for a practitioner/community-knowledge claim. The claim is not extraordinary — it's a well-understood property of non-streaming neural TTS.

4. **Outdated?** Dated 2026-01-05, current as of today (2026-06-21). Very recent.

5. **Marketing/speculation?** No. It's a critical user review, not vendor marketing. The technical observation is consistent with how these models architecturally work (non-autoregressive/chunk-based neural TTS).

The claim is well-supported, current, accurately reflects the primary source, and the source quality matches the claim's strength (a practitioner-knowledge claim).

input.evidence: The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source (espeak-ng GitHub Discussion #2338, which links to the article "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users" at stuff.interfree.ca, dated 2026-01-05). The article states verbatim: "Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text." It separately states the rationale: "Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech." Both halves of the claim (latency/restart being decisive, and these neural engines needing a full text chunk) map exactly to source text. I confirmed the quote independently via two retrievals (WebFetch of the article and a corroborating WebSearch returning identical wording). One nuance the claim correctly does NOT misrepresent: the article notes Supertonic is "slightly faster, as it can stream result audio as it becomes available," whereas Kitten cannot start speaking until the whole chunk's audio is generated — but this is about OUTPUT audio streaming; both still require a full INPUT text chunk before starting, which is precisely what the claim asserts, so there is no overreach. Checklist: (1) supported by quote — yes; (2) no contradicting credible source; (3) source quality matches a practitioner/community-knowledge claim (firsthand screen-reader power-user account on official espeak-ng org, also discussed on Hacker News, the exact community the research targets); (4) current (Jan 2026, 5 months old); (5) not marketing — it is a critical review, and the chunk-based latency behavior is consistent with the known architecture of non-streaming neural TTS.

content.text: Verification complete. The claim is NOT refuted — it is accurately and directly supported by the primary source, which I confirmed via two independent retrievals (WebFetch of the underlying article and a corroborating WebSearch returning the identical verbatim quote: "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text"). The source is a firsthand screen-reader power-user account on the official espeak-ng GitHub org, dated January 2026 (current), with appropriate quality for a community/practitioner-knowledge claim. The claim does not overreach — it correctly captures the input-text-chunk limitation without falsely denying Supertonic's output-audio streaming.
